name: Diversity
tagline: How living and non-living things are grouped and differ

diversity.title: Diversity of Living Things
keyQuestions: What are the 7 characteristics shared by all living things?
What is the difference between a vertebrate and an invertebrate?
What are the five groups of vertebrates?
How do we tell a mammal from a reptile?
What is the difference between flowering and non-flowering plants?
Why do scientists classify living things?
keyIdeas: Living things share seven characteristics: they grow, reproduce, respond to stimuli, respire, need nutrients, excrete waste, and move.
Plants are classified into flowering plants (which reproduce by producing flowers and seeds) and non-flowering plants (which reproduce by spores or cones).
Animals are classified as vertebrates (which have a backbone) or invertebrates (which do not have a backbone).
Vertebrates are divided into five groups: mammals, birds, fish, reptiles, and amphibians. Each group has distinct characteristics across skin covering, young, breathing organs, and body temperature.
Mammals have fur or hair, give birth to live young, feed young with milk, breathe with lungs, and are warm-blooded.
Fungi and bacteria are living things but are neither plants nor animals, because they do not make their own food like plants, and they do not move or eat like animals.
A single animal can share characteristics with more than one group, which can make classification difficult and requires looking at the full set of features.

glossary.definition: Any organism that carries out all 7 life processes (MRS NERG). E.g. plants, animals, fungi, bacteria.
An object that does not carry out life processes. It was never alive (e.g. rock, water) or once alive (e.g. dead wood).
An animal with a backbone (spine). Five groups: mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians, fish.
An animal without a backbone. E.g. insects, spiders, worms, crabs, snails.
glossary.template: An animal that does not have ___.

glossary.definition: Warm-blooded vertebrate with fur/hair; gives birth to live young (mostly) and feeds young with milk.
glossary.template: A vertebrate that has ___, gives birth to ___, feeds young with ___, breathes with ___, and is ___.

glossary.definition: Warm-blooded vertebrate with feathers, wings, and a beak; lays hard-shelled eggs.
glossary.template: A vertebrate that has ___, has ___, lays ___, breathes with ___, and is ___.

glossary.definition: Cold-blooded vertebrate with dry, scaly skin; lays leathery eggs on land.
glossary.template: A vertebrate that has ___, lays ___, breathes with ___, and is ___.

glossary.definition: Cold-blooded vertebrate with moist skin; lives in water and on land; lays eggs in water.
glossary.template: A vertebrate that has ___, begins life in ___ with ___, develops ___ as an adult, and is ___.

glossary.definition: Cold-blooded vertebrate; lives in water; has scales and fins; breathes through gills.
glossary.template: A vertebrate that has ___, lives in ___, breathes through ___, and is ___.

glossary.definition: An invertebrate with 3 body parts (head, thorax, abdomen), 6 legs, and usually 2 pairs of wings. E.g. ants, butterflies, bees.
glossary.template: An invertebrate that has ___ body parts and ___ legs.

glossary.definition: To group living things based on shared observable characteristics so they are easier to study and remember.
A feature of a living thing that can be observed and used to sort or identify it (e.g. number of legs, body covering).

questions.stem: A scientist found an animal with fur, lungs, and that lays eggs. Suggest why it is difficult to classify this animal into one of the five vertebrate groups.
questions.modelAnswer: The animal shares characteristics with more than one group. Its fur and lungs are characteristics of a mammal, but mammals give birth to live young, not lay eggs. The egg-laying is a characteristic of a bird or reptile, but these groups do not have fur. Because no single group matches all its features, it is difficult to classify.

questions.stem: Explain why a fish cannot survive out of water for long.
questions.modelAnswer: A fish breathes through its gills, which need to be kept wet in order to take in dissolved oxygen from the water. When a fish is out of water, the gills dry out and can no longer absorb oxygen, so the fish cannot respire and will die.

questions.stem: Ravi looked at two living things under a microscope. He saw that one made its own food and the other did not. He concluded that the one making its own food is a plant. Suggest why his conclusion may not be correct.
questions.modelAnswer: Making its own food is a characteristic of plants, but not all living things that make their own food are plants. For example, some bacteria also make their own food. To confirm that the living thing is a plant, Ravi would need to check for other plant characteristics, such as having chlorophyll in cells with cell walls.

questions.stem: Suggest one way in which a reptile is similar to a fish, and one way in which they are different.
questions.modelAnswer: Similarity: both reptiles and fish are cold-blooded, meaning their body temperature changes with the surroundings. Difference: reptiles breathe with lungs, while fish breathe through gills.

questions.stem: Explain how the way flowering plants reproduce is different from the way non-flowering plants reproduce.
questions.modelAnswer: Flowering plants reproduce by producing flowers, which undergo pollination and fertilisation to form seeds inside fruits. Non-flowering plants do not produce flowers and therefore do not produce seeds in this way; instead, they reproduce by spores (such as ferns) or by cones (such as pine trees).

misconceptions.correction: Non-living things can move (a car, a cloud). Movement is one of seven life processes; on its own it is not enough. Check for the full set (MRS NERG).

misconceptions.claim: "Whales and dolphins are fish because they live in the sea."
misconceptions.correction: They are mammals. They breathe through lungs, give birth to live young, and are warm-blooded. Habitat alone does not classify an animal.
Only iron, steel, nickel, and cobalt are magnetic. Aluminium, copper, gold, and silver are metals but NOT magnetic.

materials.title: Diversity of Materials
keyQuestions: What are the five main properties used to compare materials?
What does "hardness" mean? How is it tested?
Why is copper used for electrical wires?
What is a soluble substance? Give two everyday examples.
What is the difference between transparent, translucent, and opaque materials?
keyIdeas: Materials are classified by their properties: hardness, flexibility, strength, ability to float, waterproofness, and whether they allow light, heat, or electricity to pass through.
Materials are classified by their observable properties: hardness, flexibility, strength, water absorption, ability to transmit light, and ability to conduct electricity and heat.
A substance is soluble if it dissolves in a solvent to form a solution (e.g. sugar in water). Insoluble substances do not dissolve (e.g. sand, oil).
Materials are chosen for a job based on their properties. Copper is used for wires because it is a good electrical conductor. Rubber is used for grips because it is a good insulator.
Waterproof materials (e.g. plastic) keep water out; absorbent materials (e.g. cloth) soak it up. Choice depends on what the object has to do.

glossary.definition: How resistant a material is to being scratched or dented. Tested by trying to scratch one material with another. Metals and glass are hard; rubber is soft.
How easily a material bends without breaking. Rubber and fabric are flexible; glass and metal are generally not.
How much light passes through a material. Transparent (clear glass) lets all light through; translucent (frosted glass) lets some through; opaque (wood) lets none through.
How much water a material soaks up. Cloth and paper absorb water; plastic and glass do not.
How easily a material allows electricity to flow through it. Metals (copper, aluminium) are good conductors; rubber, plastic, and glass are insulators.
How easily a material allows heat to flow through it. Metals are good thermal conductors; wood and plastic are thermal insulators.
Whether a substance dissolves in water. Sugar and salt are soluble; sand and oil are insoluble.
The substance that dissolves (e.g. sugar or salt).
The liquid that does the dissolving (water is the most common solvent).
The mixture formed when a solute dissolves in a solvent. A solution is clear (not cloudy).
Whether a material is attracted to a magnet. Iron, steel, nickel, and cobalt are magnetic. Most other materials are non-magnetic.
How much force a material can withstand before breaking or deforming. Steel is very strong; cardboard is weak.

misconceptions.claim: "Waterproof and water-absorbent are the same thing."
misconceptions.correction: Waterproof materials block water (plastic, wax). Absorbent materials soak it up (cloth, paper). They are opposite properties.

misconceptions.claim: "Glass is a better electrical conductor than plastic because it is hard."
misconceptions.correction: Both are electrical insulators. Hardness does not decide electrical conductivity. Metals conduct; most non-metals do not.

misconceptions.claim: "Insoluble substances are not affected by water at all."
misconceptions.correction: Insoluble substances do not dissolve, but they can still get wet, mix as a suspension, or be carried along by the water.
